theory.canonical_statement: ime slots within an observation window of duration $T$. For $\Omega = 10^5$ revolutions per minute and $T = 1$ second, this contributes approximately $M_{\text{temporal}} \approx 1667$ slots or $\log_2(1667) \approx 10.7$ bits. The total information capacity thus reaches: \begin{equation} C_{\text{total}} = \log_2(153600 \times M_{\text{OAM}} \times 2 \times M_{\text{temporal}}) \approx 39 \text{ bits per photon} \end{equation} This capacity can be distributed across a volumetric display region. For a display volume of 10 centimeters cubed and an effective resolution limited by diffraction to $\Delta r = \lambda/(2 \text{NA}) \approx 180$ nanometers (assuming numerical aperture $\text{NA} = 1.4$), the total number of resolvable voxels equals: \begin{equation} N_{\text{voxels}} = \left(\frac{0.1 \text{ m}}{\Delta r}\right)^3 \approx (5.6 \times 10^5)^3 \approx 1.7 \times 10^{17} \end{equation} However, practical signal-to-noise considerations limit achievable voxel counts to approximately $10^9$ to $10^{12}$ in realistic implementations. \subsection{Fidelity Analysis and Error Metrics} The quality of encoding and reconstruction c
theory.technical_interpretation: Definition of Δ R is a scaling / order-of-magnitude relation, written Δ r = λ/(2 NA) ≈ 180. It expresses a scaling or proportionality, capturing how the quantity grows with its parameters. It is built from λ, defining Δ r. Within the Trawin Zero Point registry it serves as one element of the framework's mathematical narrative.
